content.text: Preparing data for full-model fine-tuning is fundamentally different from curating instruction pairs for supervised fine-tuning with LoRA. Instead of a few thousand high-quality conversation examples, full fine-tuning adapts every weight in the network and therefore demands a large, clean, domain-representative text corpus — typically hundreds of millions to tens of billions of tokens — processed with the same rigour applied to pretraining datasets.
What Data Prep Means for Full Fine-Tuning

Full-model fine-tuning (sometimes called continued pretraining or domain-adaptive pretraining) re-runs the standard next-token prediction objective on a new corpus after the base model has already learned general language from trillions of web tokens. The goal is to shift the model's probability distribution toward a target domain — medical literature, legal text, scientific papers, a proprietary codebase — without erasing the breadth of the original pretraining.

Unlike instruction tuning, the training examples are raw documents, not prompt-response pairs. The model reads plain text and learns to predict the next token unconditionally. This means the quality and composition of the corpus directly determines what probability distribution the model moves toward. Low-quality data (boilerplate, duplicates, off-domain noise) doesn't just fail to help — it actively degrades the model by shifting it toward the wrong distribution.

In PEFT methods like LoRA, only a small adapter is updated, and the large frozen base model provides strong regularization. Garbage data has limited reach. In full fine-tuning, every parameter moves. The signal-to-noise ratio of the corpus becomes the primary lever. A corpus that is 70% high-quality domain text and 30% low-quality noise will produce a noticeably worse model than a 95% high-quality corpus of the same size, because the noise gradients compound across all layers.

The second critical factor is domain coverage. Full fine-tuning on a single narrow corpus with no general-language data mixed in causes catastrophic forgetting — the model loses general reasoning and language capabilities as domain knowledge overwrites them. Data prep must therefore explicitly control the ratio of domain-specific to general-purpose text.
The model learns domain-specific vocabulary, abbreviations, citation conventions, and reasoning patterns that appear nowhere in web pretraining.
A model trained on real domain data assigns higher probability to correct domain facts, reducing hallucinations on domain-specific questions.
With domain-adapted weights, subsequent instruction tuning (SFT) converges faster and generalizes better within the domain.
Important facts that appear repeatedly in the domain corpus get encoded in weights, not just retrieved from context.
Standard pretraining data pipelines (tokenize, pack, shard) are well-understood and have mature tooling, unlike prompt engineering.
Full fine-tuning needs at minimum hundreds of millions of tokens to measurably shift a 7B+ model. If the domain corpus is only tens of millions of tokens, LoRA or lightweight SFT is more appropriate.
Quality filtering, deduplication at scale, and benchmark decontamination require significant engineering effort and compute (often more than the training itself).
Getting the domain-to-general mixing ratio wrong causes measurable regressions on general benchmarks — there is no safe default ratio; it must be tuned.
Domain corpora (medical papers, legal filings, proprietary code) often carry licensing restrictions. Data provenance tracking and licence audits add overhead.

A production-grade data pipeline for full fine-tuning passes raw documents through five stages: raw corpus ingestion, quality filtering, deduplication, domain mixing, and tokenization with sequence packing. Each stage applies different techniques, and their order matters — deduplication before quality filtering wastes compute, and tokenizing before mixing loses the ability to adjust ratios later.

Quality filtering removes documents that would push the language model toward undesirable distributions: very short documents (no useful signal), machine-generated boilerplate, documents dominated by non-alphabetic characters (scraped tables, ad-heavy pages), and text in the wrong language. The standard approach from C4 (the dataset behind T5) combines heuristic rules with a KenLM language model trained on high-quality reference text. Documents whose perplexity under the reference KenLM is above a threshold (typically 500 for English) are dropped — they're either garbled or belong to a very different domain.

Deduplication is the most impactful quality step for large corpora. Near-duplicate documents cause the model to memorize specific text spans rather than learning the underlying distribution. MinHash LSH with 5-gram shingles is the standard approach: documents are represented as sets of overlapping 5-word windows; two documents are near-duplicates if their estimated Jaccard similarity exceeds 0.8. Suffix array deduplication (used in The Pile and RedPajama) finds exact repeated substrings and removes them at the token level, which is more compute-intensive but catches paragraph-level repetition that MinHash misses.

Benchmark decontamination is a safety step that removes any document from the training set that contains near-duplicate text from evaluation benchmarks (MMLU questions, HumanEval problems, GSM8K problems). Without this step, the model can memorize benchmark answers during fine-tuning, producing inflated evaluation scores.

attrs.alt: Full-model fine-tuning data preparation pipeline: five stages from raw corpus through quality filtering, deduplication, domain mixing, and tokenize+pack, with a domain mixing bar chart and sequence packing illustration

content.text: Unlike instruction tuning where a few thousand high-quality examples can produce measurable improvement, full fine-tuning requires enough tokens to produce a meaningful update to all model weights. As a rule of thumb, effective domain adaptation requires at least 1 token per parameter of domain-specific text — a 7B model needs at least 7B domain tokens. In practice, the threshold is more like 5–10B tokens for noticeable gains in a 7B model, and 50–100B tokens for major capability shifts.
Easier to overfit; need careful regularization
Often more effective to use LoRA instead
When to Use Full Fine-Tuning Data Prep

Full-model fine-tuning data prep is warranted when the target domain has vocabulary, style, or factual content that is genuinely rare or absent in general pretraining data. Classic cases include specialized scientific subfields (genomics, materials science), a specific language or dialect with limited web representation, a proprietary codebase with custom APIs and conventions, or a highly regulated domain (financial filings, clinical notes) where the base model's general language understanding is insufficient.

It is generally not the right approach when: the domain corpus contains fewer than 100M tokens (PEFT is more data-efficient); the goal is only to change the model's format or instruction-following style (SFT is sufficient); or when GPU budget prohibits running multiple epochs on a billion-token dataset. The data prep overhead alone (dedup, filtering) can take days of compute — it only makes economic sense when the resulting model is expected to outlive that cost.

Training exclusively on domain text causes catastrophic forgetting — general capabilities degrade sharply because their representations are overwritten. The solution is to mix a fraction of the original pretraining data (or a high-quality general corpus like C4 or Dolma) into the fine-tuning dataset. The mixing ratio controls the forgetting-adaptation trade-off.

A common starting point is 70–80% domain-specific text, 20–30% general-purpose text. For highly specialized domains (genomics, legal), some practitioners use 90% domain with 10% general and compensate with a lower learning rate. The optimal ratio is domain-dependent and should be validated by measuring both domain-specific metrics and general benchmark scores (MMLU, HellaSwag) at the end of training.

from datasets import load_dataset, concatenate_datasets, interleave_datasets

# Load your filtered, deduplicated domain corpus
domain_ds = load_dataset(
    "arrow",
    data_files="data/domain_clean/*.arrow",
    split="train"
)

# Load a general-purpose reference corpus (C4, Dolma, etc.)
general_ds = load_dataset("allenai/c4", "en", split="train", streaming=True)

# Interleave with sampling probabilities (implements temperature mixing)
# p_domain = 0.75, p_general = 0.25
mixed_ds = interleave_datasets(
    [domain_ds, general_ds],
    probabilities=[0.75, 0.25],
    seed=42,
    stopping_strategy="all_exhausted"  # repeat shorter set until longer is done
)

print(f"Domain size: {len(domain_ds):,} examples")
# Inspect the first few examples to confirm mixing looks right
for ex in mixed_ds.take(5):
    preview = ex["text"][:100].replace("\n", " ")
    print(preview)

The pipeline below assumes a medium-scale domain corpus (~10B raw tokens) processed on a single machine with 32 CPU cores. Each step is implemented as a standalone script that reads and writes to disk, making it possible to restart from any stage if a step fails.

"""ingest.py — convert raw sources to a uniform JSONL format."""
import json, pathlib, re
from datasets import Dataset

def clean_text(raw: str) -> str:
    # Normalize Unicode to NFC, collapse whitespace
    import unicodedata
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", raw)
    text = re.sub(r"[ \t]+", " ", text)
    text = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", text)
    return text.strip()

records = []
for path in pathlib.Path("raw/").rglob("*.txt"):
    text = clean_text(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace"))
    if text:
        records.append({"text": text, "source": path.stem})

ds = Dataset.from_list(records)
ds.save_to_disk("data/01_raw")
print(f"Ingested {len(ds):,} documents")

"""quality_filter.py — C4-style heuristic filtering."""
import re
from datasets import load_from_disk

FLAGWORDS = {"lorem", "ipsum", "placeholder", "undefined", "null", "n/a"}

def heuristic_filter(doc: dict) -> bool:
    text = doc["text"]
    words = text.split()
    n_words = len(words)
    if n_words < 200 or n_words > 100_000:
        return False
    # Alphabetic character fraction
    alpha = sum(c.isalpha() for c in text)
    if alpha / max(len(text), 1) < 0.50:
        return False
    # Line-ending punctuation (at least 70% of lines)
    lines = [l.strip() for l in text.splitlines() if l.strip()]
    if not lines:
        return False
    ends_punct = sum(1 for l in lines if l[-1] in ".\'\"!?") / len(lines)
    if ends_punct < 0.40:
        return False
    # Flagword density
    flagword_count = sum(1 for w in words if w.lower() in FLAGWORDS)
    if flagword_count / n_words > 0.02:
        return False
    return True

ds = load_from_disk("data/01_raw")
before = len(ds)
ds = ds.filter(heuristic_filter, num_proc=16)
print(f"Quality filter: {before:,} → {len(ds):,} ({len(ds)/before:.1%} kept)")
ds.save_to_disk("data/02_quality")

"""dedup.py — MinHash LSH deduplication (requires: pip install datasketch)"""
from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH
from datasets import load_from_disk

JACCARD_THRESHOLD = 0.80
N_PERMUTATIONS = 128  # more permutations → higher accuracy but more memory

def get_shingles(text: str, n: int = 5) -> set:
    tokens = text.lower().split()
    return {" ".join(tokens[i:i+n]) for i in range(max(1, len(tokens)-n+1))}

ds = load_from_disk("data/02_quality")
lsh = MinHashLSH(threshold=JACCARD_THRESHOLD, num_perm=N_PERMUTATIONS)
minhashes = {}
keep_ids = set()

for i, ex in enumerate(ds):
    m = MinHash(num_perm=N_PERMUTATIONS)
    for shingle in get_shingles(ex["text"]):
        m.update(shingle.encode("utf8"))
    if not lsh.query(m):  # no near-duplicate found yet
        keep_ids.add(i)
        lsh.insert(str(i), m)
        minhashes[i] = m
    if i % 100_000 == 0:
        print(f"  processed {i:,}, kept {len(keep_ids):,}")

ds_dedup = ds.select(sorted(keep_ids))
print(f"Dedup: {len(ds):,} → {len(ds_dedup):,} ({len(ds_dedup)/len(ds):.1%} kept)")
ds_dedup.save_to_disk("data/03_dedup")

"""tokenize_pack.py — tokenize documents and pack into fixed-length sequences."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_from_disk, Dataset
import numpy as np

MODEL_NAME = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B"  # must match the model being fine-tuned
MAX_LENGTH = 4096

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
ds = load_from_disk("data/03_dedup")

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(
        batch["text"],
        truncation=False,
        padding=False,
        return_attention_mask=False,
    )

tokenized = ds.map(tokenize, batched=True, num_proc=16, remove_columns=["text", "source"])

# Pack tokens: concatenate with EOS separator, then chunk into MAX_LENGTH
def pack_sequences(examples):
    all_ids = []
    for ids in examples["input_ids"]:
        all_ids.extend(ids)
        all_ids.append(tokenizer.eos_token_id)  # EOS between documents

    # Chunk into MAX_LENGTH windows
    chunks = [
        all_ids[i:i + MAX_LENGTH]
        for i in range(0, len(all_ids) - MAX_LENGTH + 1, MAX_LENGTH)
    ]
    return {"input_ids": chunks}

packed = tokenized.map(
    pack_sequences,
    batched=True,
    batch_size=2048,  # large batch to improve packing efficiency
    remove_columns=tokenized.column_names,
    num_proc=8,
)

print(f"Packed dataset: {len(packed):,} sequences of {MAX_LENGTH} tokens")
print(f"Total tokens: {len(packed) * MAX_LENGTH:,}")
packed.save_to_disk("data/04_packed")

Consider adapting Llama-3 8B to medical literature for a clinical decision support application. The target corpus consists of 5M PubMed abstracts (≈3B tokens) and 200k clinical guidelines (≈500M tokens). After quality filtering (removing very short abstracts, non-English text, retracted articles), the corpus shrinks to about 2.7B domain tokens.

To prevent forgetting of general reasoning, 25% of the training mix is drawn from a filtered C4 slice (general English web text). Benchmark decontamination removes any PubMed abstract that appears in MedQA or MedMCQA evaluation sets — roughly 0.3% of documents. The final training dataset is 3.6B tokens, packed into 877,440 sequences of 4096 tokens.

# benchmark_decontam.py — remove training docs that overlap eval benchmarks
from datasets import load_dataset, load_from_disk
from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH

# Build LSH index from all evaluation benchmark text
eval_ds = load_dataset("bigbio/med_qa", split="test")
lsh = MinHashLSH(threshold=0.70, num_perm=64)

for i, ex in enumerate(eval_ds):
    m = MinHash(num_perm=64)
    for shingle in ex["question"].lower().split():
        m.update(shingle.encode())
    lsh.insert(f"eval_{i}", m)

# Filter training corpus: remove docs that match any eval document
train_ds = load_from_disk("data/02_quality")

def is_not_contaminated(doc):
    m = MinHash(num_perm=64)
    for shingle in doc["text"][:2000].lower().split():
        m.update(shingle.encode())
    return len(lsh.query(m)) == 0

train_clean = train_ds.filter(is_not_contaminated, num_proc=16)
print(f"Removed {len(train_ds) - len(train_clean):,} contaminated documents")
Model memorizes repeated spans; inflated eval on contaminated benchmarks
MinHash + suffix array dedup; benchmark decontam
Catastrophic forgetting; model fails at arithmetic, reasoning, instruction following
Token IDs don't match model's embedding table; training is random noise
Always load tokenizer from same checkpoint as the model being fine-tuned
GPU utilization 50–70%; 1.5–2× slower; wasted compute on padding tokens
Pack documents with EOS between them; set attention mask via position_ids
Model learns garbled character sequences; perplexity improves on garbage
Filter on alphabetic character fraction > 0.5; inspect data visually
Model learns to only respond to the specific prompt template; poor open-ended generation
Use raw continuation format for domain adaptation; apply instruction tuning separately afterward
Randomly sample 500 documents from each pipeline stage and read them. Automated metrics miss structured patterns like boilerplate, OCR errors, and encoding artifacts.
Save every intermediate stage to disk with a version tag. Iterating on the filtering threshold for one stage should not require re-running earlier stages.
Measure token count before committing to full training:
After packing, count total tokens. If the domain tokens are fewer than 1× the model's parameter count, reconsider whether full fine-tuning is the right approach.
Use the exact same tokenizer (including special tokens and BOS/EOS behavior) as the checkpoint you're fine-tuning. Different tokenizers produce different token IDs for the same text.
Run a 1–2 hour training pilot on 1% of the dataset to validate the data pipeline, confirm loss decreases, and detect obvious data problems before committing to the full run.
Record the source field for every document. When a fine-tuned model exhibits unexpected behavior, provenance tracking allows you to find and remove the responsible documents.

MinHash deduplication on a 10B token corpus (≈3–5M documents) takes 2–4 hours on a 32-core machine with Python's datasketch. For 100B tokens, distributed processing is required. The text-dedup and datatrove libraries support distributed deduplication via Spark or Ray, partitioning documents into buckets based on hash prefix.

Tokenization is CPU-bound and embarrassingly parallel. The HuggingFace datasets library's `map` function with `num_proc=N` scales linearly up to available cores. Packing adds a small sequential overhead (concatenation and chunking) but is also parallelizable with large batch sizes. A 10B-token corpus typically tokenizes and packs in under 30 minutes on 32 cores.

Arrow format (the default for HuggingFace datasets saved to disk) provides memory-mapped access, meaning the training loop can stream data from disk without loading the full dataset into RAM. This is critical for billion-token datasets. Sharding the dataset into 64–256 files enables parallel I/O during training and simplifies restarting from a checkpoint.

# Shard the packed dataset for efficient parallel I/O during training
from datasets import load_from_disk

packed = load_from_disk("data/04_packed")
N_SHARDS = 128

for i in range(N_SHARDS):
    shard = packed.shard(num_shards=N_SHARDS, index=i, contiguous=True)
    shard.to_parquet(f"data/05_sharded/shard_{i:04d}.parquet")
    if i % 16 == 0:
        print(f"Sharded {i}/{N_SHARDS}")

print("Sharding complete — ready for distributed training")
HuggingFace library; used to process FineWeb
n-gram LM; fast inference; used by C4 pipeline
HuggingFace dataset; excellent general mix source

Domain corpora collected from the web or internal sources carry several risks that must be managed during data preparation. PII (personally identifiable information) — names, email addresses, phone numbers, medical record numbers — can be memorized during training and later regurgitated in model outputs. Run a PII detection pass using tools like Microsoft Presidio or spaCy's NER pipeline to redact or pseudonymize sensitive fields before training.

Copyright infringement is a significant legal risk for commercial deployments. Track the licence and source of every document in the corpus. Documents under non-commercial licences (CC BY-NC) cannot be used to train a model intended for commercial deployment. Maintain a provenance database mapping each training document to its original URL, collection date, and licence, so that removal requests can be complied with without reprocessing the full corpus.

Poisoning attacks are a concern when data is collected from user-controlled sources. A malicious actor who can inject text into the training corpus can influence model behavior. Mitigations include collecting from trusted, authenticated sources, running toxicity classifiers, and auditing the highest-weight documents (those seen many times due to duplication) specifically.

Think of the base model as a highly educated generalist who has read most of the internet. Full fine-tuning is like enrolling that person in a full-time graduate program in your domain — they read domain papers, textbooks, and case studies for months. The quality of the curriculum (the data) determines how much they actually learn. If the reading list is full of blog summaries, duplicate articles, and off-topic content, they don't gain real expertise — they gain a shallow familiarity. If the reading list is curated, diverse, and deduplicated, they develop genuine depth. The domain mixing ratio is like ensuring they still read newspapers and general interest books alongside the domain material, so they don't lose their general reasoning while specializing.
Full fine-tuning requires significantly more data than LoRA — at least 1B domain tokens for a 7B model, ideally 5–50B — because all weights are updated simultaneously.
The five-stage pipeline (ingest → quality filter → dedup → domain mix → tokenize/pack) is the production standard; skipping any stage measurably degrades the resulting model.
MinHash LSH deduplication with a Jaccard threshold of 0.8 and 5-gram shingles is the practical baseline; supplement with benchmark decontamination to prevent eval inflation.
Always mix 20–30% general-purpose text (C4, FineWeb) with domain data to prevent catastrophic forgetting of general reasoning capabilities.
Sequence packing (concatenating documents with EOS separators) raises GPU utilization from ~60% (padded) to ~98% (packed), making training 1.5–2× more efficient.
Always tokenize with the exact same tokenizer checkpoint as the model being fine-tuned — mismatched tokenizers produce undefined, broken training.
Track data provenance (source, URL, licence, collection date) for every document — it is required for copyright compliance and enables targeted removal without reprocessing the corpus.
Run a 1–2 hour pilot on 1% of the packed dataset before committing to a full training run — this catches data pipeline bugs at a fraction of the cost.
